questions.question: Order the steps of a leakage-safe supervised ML workflow.
items: Train the model to fit its parameters
Split the data into train, validation, and test
Compare models with a statistical test
Fit data-driven preprocessing inside each fold
Tune hyperparameters on validation / cross-validation
Evaluate once on the held-out test set
correct_order: Split the data into train, validation, and test
Fit data-driven preprocessing inside each fold
Train the model to fit its parameters
Tune hyperparameters on validation / cross-validation
Evaluate once on the held-out test set
Compare models with a statistical test
questions.explanation: Splitting first is the golden rule; preprocessing is fit inside each fold, the model is trained, hyperparameters are tuned on validation, the test set is used exactly once, and only then are models compared with a statistical test.

questions.question: Order the steps for correctly applying SMOTE on an imbalanced dataset.
items: Split the data before any resampling
Apply SMOTE only to the training fold
Train the classifier on the resampled training data
Evaluate on the untouched test data
correct_order: Split the data before any resampling
Apply SMOTE only to the training fold
Train the classifier on the resampled training data
Evaluate on the untouched test data
questions.explanation: SMOTE must come after the split and inside the training fold so synthetic points never use test rows; performance may honestly drop once the leakage is removed.

questions.question: Order the steps for setting and using a classification decision threshold without leakage.
items: Train the model on the training data
Calibrate the decision threshold on training only
Lock the threshold before touching the test set
Apply the fixed threshold to the test predictions
correct_order: Train the model on the training data
Calibrate the decision threshold on training only
Lock the threshold before touching the test set
Apply the fixed threshold to the test predictions
questions.explanation: The threshold is a hyperparameter chosen from training data and then locked; re-tuning it on the test set (for example, 0.57 on train but 0.49 on test) inflates the reported estimate.

questions.question: Order the steps that fix a tree model which cannot extrapolate on a rising trend.
items: Notice predictions flatten and never exceed past values on a rising trend
Recognize that trees output averages of values seen in training
Transform the problem to predict the difference or residual off a trend
Fit the model on the transformed target and forecast the trend
correct_order: Notice predictions flatten and never exceed past values on a rising trend
Recognize that trees output averages of values seen in training
Transform the problem to predict the difference or residual off a trend
Fit the model on the transformed target and forecast the trend
questions.explanation: Once you see flattening and understand trees cannot extrapolate, you reframe the target as a difference or residual (or use a linear booster) so the model can follow the rising trend.

questions.question: Order the steps of a transparent confirmatory behavioural study.
items: Pre-register the confirmatory hypotheses and analysis plan
Collect the data as planned
Run the pre-planned confirmatory analysis
Label any extra findings as exploratory and report everything
Share the data and code for reproducibility
correct_order: Pre-register the confirmatory hypotheses and analysis plan
Collect the data as planned
Run the pre-planned confirmatory analysis
Label any extra findings as exploratory and report everything
Share the data and code for reproducibility
questions.explanation: Pre-registering before data collection guards against HARKing and p-hacking; running the planned analysis, labelling unplanned findings as exploratory, and sharing data and code complete a transparent, reproducible study.
